claims.claim_text: The opening warehouse rollout story is presented like a concrete real incident but has no attributable source.
claims.span_hint: section: Why This Module Matters | paragraph 1
claims.anchor_text: The release manager was not trying to build a registry platform. She was trying to ship a security fix to a fleet of small warehouse clusters before the night shift started. The central registry was healthy, the CI pipeline had produced the image, and the deployment manifest was correct. The rollout still failed because each warehouse tried to pull the same large base image across a slow shared link, and half the sites timed out before the first Pod became ready.
claims.suggested_rewrite: A team may not be trying to build a registry platform at all; it may simply need to ship a fix to remote clusters over constrained links. Even when the central registry, CI pipeline, and deployment manifest are correct, a rollout can still fail if many sites pull the same large image over a slow shared link.
claims.rationale: This is an unsourced composite anecdote framed as a real operational event, so it should be generalized rather than cited.

claims.claim_text: The follow-on comparison to a headquarters registry stack continues the same unsourced concrete incident narrative.
claims.span_hint: section: Why This Module Matters | paragraph 2
claims.anchor_text: The first proposed fix was to install the same enterprise registry used at headquarters. That looked reasonable on an architecture diagram, but it collapsed under operational reality. The warehouse devices had limited memory, no database administrator, and intermittent connectivity. A registry with several services, a database, a job runner, and a web UI was more platform than the edge needed. The teams needed local OCI distribution, not another system to babysit.
claims.suggested_rewrite: One response would be to install a larger enterprise registry stack at the edge, but that can be a poor fit when devices have limited memory, connectivity is intermittent, and there is no staff to maintain several supporting services. In that kind of environment, teams may only need local OCI distribution rather than a heavier registry platform.
claims.rationale: The operational lesson is plausible, but the paragraph reads like a sourced case study without any attributable source.

claims.claim_text: OCI artifacts other than runnable container images can use the OCI image manifest model, and related artifacts can be associated to a subject digest via referrers.
claims.rationale: The OCI image manifest spec explicitly covers artifact usage beyond images and the `subject` linkage used by referrers.

claims.claim_text: Zot's documented configuration supports filesystem and S3 storage, authentication and authorization, sync, metrics, and Trivy-backed CVE search.
claims.span_hint: section: Why This Module Matters | paragraphs 3-4 and section 4 config discussion
claims.rationale: The upstream configuration guide documents these concrete Zot capabilities directly.

claims.claim_text: The sentence claiming Zot can run as one process and do so without requiring a database overreaches what the cited allowlisted docs state explicitly.
claims.span_hint: section: Why This Module Matters | paragraph 3
claims.anchor_text: It is a small, OCI-native registry that can run as one process, store content on a filesystem or object store, mirror upstream registries, expose metrics, and handle common artifact types without requiring a database.
claims.suggested_rewrite: It is a small, OCI-native registry that can store content on a filesystem or object store, mirror upstream registries, expose metrics, and handle common artifact types with a deliberately small operational footprint.
claims.rationale: The upstream allowlisted pages clearly support the storage, sync, and metrics features, but not this exact one-process/no-database phrasing.

claims.claim_text: OCI Distribution registries use `/v2/` to determine API support, pulls use manifest and blob GET/HEAD operations, and pushes upload blobs before the final manifest write.
claims.span_hint: section: Trace Zot from Client Request to Storage | opening paragraphs and endpoint table
claims.rationale: The OCI Distribution Specification directly defines `/v2/`, blob and manifest endpoints, and the push ordering.

claims.claim_text: Zot supports deduplication for shared blobs on compatible filesystems, multiple storage subpaths, and S3 storage drivers.
claims.span_hint: section: Trace Zot from Client Request to Storage | dedup paragraph and section 4 storage discussion
claims.rationale: The Zot configuration examples explicitly describe `dedupe`, `subPaths`, and S3-backed storage.

claims.claim_text: The module's pinned Zot release `v2.1.14` is a real published release.
claims.span_hint: section: Deploy Zot from the Smallest Useful Configuration | first code example intro
claims.rationale: The official Zot GitHub releases page shows a published `v2.1.14` release.

claims.claim_text: Docker treats loopback registries specially and otherwise requires insecure-registry configuration for plain-HTTP registries.
claims.span_hint: section: Deploy Zot from the Smallest Useful Configuration | paragraph after local run example
claims.rationale: This is a real Docker daemon behavior, but the authoritative page is on `docs.docker.com`, which is off the current allowlist.

claims.claim_text: Zot supports local htpasswd auth, LDAP auth, OpenID/OAuth2 providers, and repository-prefix authorization policies with actions such as read, create, update, and delete.
claims.span_hint: section: Configure Storage, Authentication, and Policy Deliberately | auth and policy discussion
claims.rationale: The Zot examples guide documents these authentication backends and authorization actions directly.

claims.claim_text: Zot sync supports on-demand pulls, periodic polling via `pollInterval`, and repository-prefix/tag filtering, which is the basis for the module's cache-versus-prewarm distinction.
claims.span_hint: section: Mirror, Cache, and Move OCI Artifacts | on-demand cache and scheduled mirroring discussion
claims.rationale: The sync configuration docs explicitly define `onDemand`, `pollInterval`, and content filters, which directly support the operational distinction drawn in the text.

claims.claim_text: Helm can push packaged charts to OCI registries and pull them back using `oci://` references.
claims.span_hint: section: Mirror, Cache, and Move OCI Artifacts | Helm paragraph
claims.rationale: The Helm OCI registries documentation directly shows `helm push` and `helm pull` against OCI registries.

claims.claim_text: Cosign stores signatures in OCI registries and recommends signing image digests rather than mutable tags.
claims.span_hint: section: Mirror, Cache, and Move OCI Artifacts | cosign paragraph
claims.rationale: The upstream Cosign README states that signatures are stored in OCI registries and explicitly advises signing by digest, not tag.

claims.claim_text: Tags are mutable references while digests identify immutable image content.
claims.span_hint: section: Trace Zot from Client Request to Storage | worked example paragraph and Did You Know item 2
claims.rationale: The Kubernetes images documentation directly explains that tags can move while digests are immutable content identifiers.

claims.claim_text: The later 'successful warehouse design' paragraph resumes the same unsourced narrative as if it were an actual case study.
claims.span_hint: section: Operate Zot Under Failure | warehouse story paragraph near end
claims.anchor_text: Consider the warehouse story from the opening. The successful design used a central registry as the source of truth and local Zot instances as site caches. Critical images were pre-warmed before planned rollouts, and on-demand mirroring handled less common images. The local clusters pulled from the local registry so that repeated restarts did not repeatedly cross the wide-area link. When a site lost connectivity, existing workloads could still restart because the needed images were already local.
claims.suggested_rewrite: In this kind of environment, a common design is to keep a central registry as the source of truth and use local registries as site caches. Critical images can be pre-warmed before planned rollouts, while on-demand mirroring handles less common images. That reduces repeated pulls across the wide-area link and improves restart resilience when connectivity is lost.
claims.rationale: This is still a composite operational scenario, so it should be rewritten as general design guidance rather than left as an implied real case.

further_reading.title: Zot Configuration Examples
further_reading.why_relevant: Primary upstream reference for Zot storage, auth, authorization, sync, metrics, and CVE-search configuration.

further_reading.title: OCI Distribution Specification
further_reading.why_relevant: Authoritative spec for `/v2/`, blob and manifest endpoints, push/pull flow, and referrers behavior.

further_reading.title: OCI Image Manifest and Artifact Guidance
further_reading.why_relevant: Best primary source for how OCI artifacts, `artifactType`, and `subject`/referrers relationships work.

further_reading.title: Helm OCI Registries
further_reading.why_relevant: Direct Helm documentation for storing charts in OCI registries, which is a core artifact example in the module.

notes: Ignored quiz items, hands-on exercise steps, code blocks, and most pedagogical diagrams per prompt. Consolidated repeated hard claims such as tag-vs-digest identity and on-demand-cache limitations to their first substantive occurrence.
